questions.question: Explain the producer-consumer problem and why simple shared variable access without synchronization is insufficient.
keyPoints: Define producer-consumer: producers generate data, consumers process it
Bounded buffer creates coordination challenge
Race conditions without synchronization lead to data corruption
Buffer overflow (produce into full) and underflow (consume from empty) are possible
Example: two producers incrementing shared counter may lose one update

questions.question: Draw and explain the state diagram for a bounded buffer with N slots, showing transitions between empty, partially filled, and full states.
keyPoints: Three states: Empty (0 items), Partial (1 to N-1 items), Full (N items)
Transitions: Empty→Partial (produce), Partial→Full (produce), Full→Partial (consume), Partial→Empty (consume)
Synchronization prevents invalid transitions
Atomic operations needed for state transitions

questions.question: Explain the role of semaphores in solving the producer-consumer problem. What are the initial values and what does signal/wait accomplish for each?
keyPoints: Empty semaphore: counts empty slots, initialized to N (buffer size)
Full semaphore: counts filled slots, initialized to 0
wait(empty): producer decrements empty count, blocks if zero
signal(empty): consumer increments empty count after removing item
wait(full): consumer decrements full count, blocks if zero
signal(full): producer increments full count after adding item

questions.question: What is false sharing in the context of producer-consumer, and how does it affect performance even with correct synchronization?
keyPoints: False sharing: different threads accessing different data on same cache line
Producer and consumer accessing adjacent buffer slots may share cache line
Cache line invalidation causes extra memory traffic
Performance degradation can be 10-100x despite correct logical behavior
Solution: pad data structures or align to cache line boundaries

questions.question: Derive the throughput for a system with 5 producers (each producing at 80 items/sec) and 3 consumers (each consuming at 100 items/sec).
keyPoints: Total production rate = 5 × 80 = 400 items/sec
Total consumption rate = 3 × 100 = 300 items/sec
Throughput = min(production_rate, consumption_rate) = 300 items/sec
Bottleneck is consumer side (300 < 400)
If all consumers busy, producers may still be blocked when buffer fills

questions.question: Compare blocking vs non-blocking producer-consumer implementations. When would you choose each?
keyPoints: Blocking: threads sleep using condition variables/semaphores when buffer full/empty
Non-blocking: returns immediately with status (EWOULDBLOCK)
Blocking: simpler, lower CPU usage, better for general use
Non-blocking: better for real-time, event-driven systems, avoids deadlock in certain patterns
Blocking preferred for most applications; non-blocking for specialized scenarios

questions.question: Design and implement a producer-consumer solution using Pthreads with mutex and condition variables. Explain each component and verify correctness.
keyPoints: Define shared buffer structure with array, size, head, tail indices
Initialize mutex for critical section protection
Initialize two condition variables: not_full, not_empty
Producer: lock mutex → while buffer full wait(not_full) → add item → signal(not_empty) → unlock
Consumer: lock mutex → while buffer empty wait(not_empty) → remove item → signal(not_full) → unlock
Explain why while loops needed (spurious wakeups)
Discuss deadlock prevention (correct lock ordering)

questions.question: A pipeline system has three stages: Stage1 (producers), Stage2 (middle processors), Stage3 (consumers). Stage1 produces at 200/sec, Stage2 processes at 150/sec, Stage3 consumes at 180/sec. With bounded buffers of size 100 between each stage, analyze the system behavior and determine steady-state throughput.
keyPoints: Stage1→Stage2: production=200, consumption=150, bottleneck rate=150
Stage2→Stage3: production=150, consumption=180, bottleneck rate=150
Overall throughput determined by slowest stage = 150 items/sec
Buffer1 fills at rate 200-150=50/sec initially until full (100 slots → 2 seconds)
Buffer2 drains at rate 180-150=30/sec initially until empty (100 slots → 3.33 seconds)
After transient: system stabilizes at 150 throughput with periodic blocking
Explain back-pressure propagation from slowest stage

questions.question: Implement a lock-free single-producer single-consumer circular buffer using compare-and-swap (CAS). Explain how you handle the challenges of atomic index updates and ensure correctness.
keyPoints: Use two atomic indices: write_idx, read_idx
Producer: load read_idx atomically, calculate available = (write - read + capacity) mod capacity
If available < capacity: CAS write_idx from old to (old+1) mod capacity, write data
Consumer: similar but checks if (write - read) > 0
ABA problem: may need tagged pointers or version counters
Explain why SPSC sufficient (no multi-producer race on same slot)
Compare with blocking version: pros/cons of busy-wait vs sleep
